28: A direct revelation: 'Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the Holy Spirit has made you overseers, to care for the church of God, which he obtained with his own blood.' The theological density of this charge to the Ephesian elders is unparalleled in Acts: (1) the Spirit appoints church leadership; (2) the church belongs to God himself; (3) God purchased this church through blood that is 'his own'. Whether tou haimatos tou idiou is rendered 'his own blood' (ascribing blood to God the Father, implying the Son's blood is God's own) or 'the blood of his own [Son]', the Christological purchase is irreversible and costly beyond measure. The cross is the price God paid for his own community.
1: Paul's departure from Ephesus — after the riot and three years of ministry — begins the journey toward Jerusalem that Acts has been building toward since Acts 19:21. The journey pattern mirrors Jesus's own departure from Galilee toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51 — 'he set his face to go to Jerusalem'). The Spirit-driven directionality of Paul's journey (Acts 20:22 — 'bound in the Spirit I am going to Jerusalem') echoes the divine necessity of Jesus's journey: 'the Son of Man must suffer many things' (Luke 9:22).
2: The Macedonian revisit — encouraging the believers with 'much exhortation' (paraklēsei pollē) — is pastoral consolation in the pattern of Jesus's farewell discourses (John 14–17). The risen Lord's mission includes the sustained nurture of planted communities, not only initial proclamation. Paul's extended encouragement mirrors Jesus's extended private teaching of the Twelve before his passion journey: the teacher prepares his community for his absence.
3: The Jewish plot against Paul at Corinth — triggering the return through Macedonia — begins the passion-pattern foreshadowing that will intensify through chapters 21–28. Jesus had forewarned: 'they will hand you over to councils and you will be beaten in synagogues' (Mark 13:9). The plot's detection and Paul's route change are providential protection — the same kind of divine intervention that enabled Jesus to move safely between Jerusalem and Galilee until 'his hour had come' (John 7:30; Acts 8:20).
4: The collection delegates — seven men from across the Gentile mission communities — accompanying Paul to Jerusalem represent the Gentile church's solidarity with the Jewish mother community. Jesus had said: 'by this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another' (John 13:35). The collection is an act of love from those who have received the gospel through Israel toward those who first received and transmitted it — the body of Christ expressing its unity across ethnic and geographical lines.
5: The resumption of 'we' at Philippi — after the gap since Acts 16:17 — reconnects Luke's eyewitness presence to the narrative at the point where Paul's Jerusalem journey intensifies. The 'we' sections of Acts (Acts 16:10–17; Acts 20:5–Acts 21:18; Acts 27:1–Acts 28:16) all cluster around maritime and Jerusalem journeys — the most vulnerable and significant phases of the mission. Luke's presence with Paul in these sections is his testimony to the events that shaped the early church's defining journey.
6: The departure after the days of Unleavened Bread — the week of Passover — connects Paul's Jerusalem journey to the Passover season in which Jesus died. The timing is not coincidental in Luke's theology: Paul's passion journey to Jerusalem unfolds in the liturgical season of Israel's liberation, the season of Jesus's death and resurrection. The gospel's founding event and Paul's passion-journey shadow move in the same liturgical time.
7: 'On the first day of the week, when we were gathered together to break bread' — the first explicit attestation in Acts of Sunday (the day of resurrection) as the community's primary gathering day for the breaking of bread (the Eucharist). Jesus had appeared to the disciples on the first day (Luke 24:1; John 20:1, 19) and 'was known to them in the breaking of bread' (Luke 24:35). The community gathers on resurrection day to enact the resurrection meal — the risen Lord present in the breaking of bread that the disciples recognized at Emmaus.
8: The many lamps in the upper room create the physical setting for the Eucharistic gathering — and the accident that follows. The upper room (hyperōon) is the same space as Jerusalem's upper room (Acts 1:13) where the disciples had gathered after the resurrection. The domestic, lamplight setting of the Eucharistic community in Troas echoes both the upper room of the Last Supper (Luke 22:12) and the resurrection appearances.
9: Eutychus's fall from the window — 'sinking into deep sleep as Paul talked still longer' — and his apparent death creates the occasion for a death-and-resurrection miracle in the Eucharistic gathering. The young man falls during the proclamation of the word and during the breaking of bread — the two elements of the early Christian gathering (Acts 2:42: 'the apostles' teaching and the fellowship, to the breaking of bread'). His fall is the death that the gathering's theology addresses.
10: Paul's 'falling on him and embracing him' deliberately echoes Elijah's action over the widow's son (1 Kings 17:21) and Elisha's over the Shunammite's son (2 Kings 4:34). The prophetic embodiment of resurrection — the living prophet lying on the dead body, transferring life — is the OT pattern that Paul enacts. But behind the OT pattern is the risen Christ who is the source of all resurrection power: Paul acts as agent of the Lord who is 'the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25).
11: After the miracle, Paul 'broke bread and ate' — the Eucharistic meal proceeds after the resurrection sign. The sequence is theologically exact: death encountered (Eutychus falls), life restored (Paul's embrace), bread broken (the resurrection meal eaten). The Eucharist is the ritual enactment of what has just happened visibly: death is overcome, life is restored, and the community eats together in the presence of the risen Lord.
12: The community 'took the youth away alive' and 'were not a little comforted' (pareклēthēsan) — the verb is parakaleo, the same root as the Paraclete (Helper) Jesus promised (John 14:16). The comfort the community receives after the death-and-resurrection miracle is the Spirit's ministry: the Comforter works through the enacted sign to strengthen the community's faith. The gathering that began with the breaking of bread and discourse ends with a living demonstration of resurrection.
13: Paul walks from Troas to Assos (about 30 km on foot) while the ship takes the sea route. The deliberate separation — Paul alone on foot, companions by sea — creates a space of solitude before the final approach to Jerusalem, analogous to Jesus's withdrawal to pray before his passion (Luke 22:39–46). The one who walks to meet his suffering does so with deliberate solitude, not accidentally.
14: The coastal voyage names — Assos, Mitylene, Chios, Samos, Miletus — are the geography of Paul's final approach to Jerusalem, each step bringing him closer to the city where suffering awaits. Jesus's journey to Jerusalem was similarly staged (Luke 9:51–Acts 19:44), with each stopping point a preparation for what lay ahead. Luke's attention to the precise itinerary mirrors his attention to Jesus's journey narrative in Luke 9–19.
15: The deliberate route — bypassing Ephesus — is Paul's pastoral decision protecting the mission's momentum: a Jerusalem arrival for Pentecost over a pastoral detour in Ephesus. Jesus had also made deliberate routing decisions (avoiding certain regions, taking others) based on the mission's timing and his awareness of coming events. The risen Lord's servant navigates geography with theological purposefulness.
16: The urgency to be in Jerusalem for Pentecost — fifty days after Passover — places Paul's Jerusalem arrival in the same liturgical season as the Spirit's original outpouring. If Paul arrives as planned, he will be in Jerusalem at Pentecost: the day when the Spirit first fell (Acts 2), the day Paul's collection arrives, the day his Jerusalem trials begin. The liturgical resonance is the risen Lord's own calendar at work in the mission.
17: The summons of the Ephesian elders to Miletus begins Paul's most sustained and intimate pastoral speech in Acts. The word 'elders' (presbyterous) — those who shepherd the community — mirrors Jesus's commissioning of the Twelve as shepherds of Israel (Matthew 10:6; Luke 22:26). Paul's farewell speech models what Jesus modeled in the upper room discourses (John 14–17): the departing leader preparing his community for his absence.
18: 'You yourselves know how I lived among you the whole time from the first day that I set foot in Asia.' Paul's appeal to community memory as the ground of his testimony echoes Jesus's appeal to his disciples: 'you are witnesses of these things' (Luke 24:48). The community's shared memory of the leader's life is the verification of his character — not external credentials but observed conduct. Paul's self-commendation through community memory mirrors Jesus's 'what have you seen and heard?' approach to testimony.
19: 'Serving the Lord with all humility and with tears and with trials through the plots of the Jews' — the combination of humility, tears, and suffering is the passion portrait of the Lord himself. Jesus had said: 'learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart' (Matthew 11:29); he wept over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) and in Gethsemane. Paul's ministry form mirrors his Lord's: humble service, tears of pastoral investment, suffering through opposition.
20: 'I did not shrink from declaring to you anything that was profitable, and teaching you in public and from house to house' — the dual venue mirrors Jesus's own teaching pattern: synagogue (public) and house (private, Matthew 13:36; Mark 7:17; Luke 10:38–42). The teacher who does not shrink from difficult truths follows Jesus's example: 'I have spoken openly to the world... I have said nothing in secret' (John 18:20). Faithful proclamation in all venues is the mark of the Lord's servant.
21: 'Testifying both to Jews and to Greeks of repentance toward God and of faith in our Lord Jesus Christ' — the bipartite gospel formula: repentance (metanoia) and faith in Jesus Christ. Jesus's own inaugural proclamation had been: 'Repent and believe in the gospel' (Mark 1:15); Luke's opening of Jesus's Galilean ministry was: 'The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe in the gospel.' Paul proclaims the same call the risen Lord first made, extended now to both Jews and Greeks.
22: 'And now, behold, I am going to Jerusalem, constrained by the Spirit, not knowing what will happen to me there.' The Spirit's constraint (dedemenos tō pneumati — bound in the Spirit) is the divine necessity language Jesus used of his own passion: 'must the Christ not suffer these things?' (Luke 24:26). Paul's Spirit-bound journey to Jerusalem under uncertain prospects mirrors Jesus's Spirit-led journey to Jerusalem under certain passion prediction. The risen Lord shapes his servant's journey in the pattern of his own.
23: 'Except that the Holy Spirit testifies to me in every city that imprisonment and afflictions await me' — the Spirit's forewarning of suffering in every city parallels Jesus's repeated passion predictions to his disciples (Luke 9:22; Acts 9:44; Acts 18:31–33). The prediction of suffering before entry into glory is the consistent pattern: 'Was it not necessary that the Christ should suffer these things and enter into his glory?' (Luke 24:26). Paul knows what awaits; he goes anyway — Gethsemane-like faithfulness.
24: 'But I do not account my life of any value to myself, if only I may finish my course and the ministry that I received from the Lord Jesus, to testify to the gospel of the grace of God.' Paul's self-giving mirrors Jesus's own self-emptying: 'the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many' (Mark 10:45). The ministry received from the Lord Jesus is discharged in the Lord Jesus's own pattern: life held loosely, mission held firmly, grace proclaimed to the end.
25: 'And now, behold, I know that none of you among whom I have gone about proclaiming the kingdom will see my face again.' Paul's farewell prophecy of final departure echoes Jesus's farewell discourse prediction: 'A little while and you will see me no longer' (John 16:16). The departing Lord prepares his disciples for his absence while promising the Spirit's continued presence; Paul prepares the Ephesian elders for his absence while commending them to 'God and to the word of his grace' (Acts 20:32). The pattern of departure, preparation, and continued presence through the word is the passion-resurrection pattern extended into apostolic ministry.
26: 'Therefore I testify to you this day that I am innocent of the blood of all, for I did not shrink from declaring to you the whole counsel of God.' Paul's innocence of blood echoes Ezekiel's watchman theology (Ezekiel 33:7–9) but also Jesus's own claim of faithfulness to the Father's word: 'I have not spoken on my own authority, but the Father who sent me has himself given me a commandment — what to say and what to speak' (John 12:49). The faithful proclaimer is innocent of the consequences of rejection.
27: 'The whole counsel of God' (pasan tēn boulēn tou theou) — the comprehensive divine plan that Paul has proclaimed without suppression. Jesus had declared: 'I have made known to you everything that I heard from my Father' (John 15:15). The servant who fully declares the master's word mirrors the Son who fully reveals the Father's will. Partial proclamation — suppressing difficult truths to avoid offense — fails both the Lord and the hearers.
29: 'I know that after my departure fierce wolves will come in among you, not sparing the flock' — the wolf-flock imagery invokes the Good Shepherd's warning: 'I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep. He who is a hired hand and not a shepherd... sees the wolf coming and leaves the sheep and flees, and the wolf snatches them' (John 10:11–12). Paul's warning that he is departing while wolves will come is the pastoral corollary: the undershepherds must do what the Good Shepherd modeled — guard the flock at the cost of their own comfort.
30: 'From among your own selves men will arise speaking twisted things, to draw away the disciples after them.' The internal false teacher as the greater threat echoes Jesus's warning about false prophets: 'Beware of false prophets, who come to you in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravenous wolves' (Matthew 7:15). The internal origin — 'from among your own selves' — makes the threat harder to detect and more damaging: the wolf in shepherd's clothing is harder to identify than the external predator.
31: 'Therefore be alert, remembering that for three years I did not cease night or day to admonish every one with tears.' The call to alertness (grēgoreite) echoes Jesus's Gethsemane command to the disciples: 'Watch and pray that you may not enter into temptation' (Matthew 26:41). The disciples failed to watch at Gethsemane; the Ephesian elders must not repeat the failure. Paul's three years of tears-filled admonition models what the risen Lord requires of those who tend his flock: not casual oversight but intense, costly vigilance.
32: 'And now I commend you to God and to the word of his grace, which is able to build you up and to give you the inheritance among all those who are sanctified.' The commendation to God and the word echoes Jesus's high priestly prayer: 'I am no longer in the world, but they are in the world, and I am coming to you. Holy Father, keep them in your name' (John 17:11). The departing servant cannot keep the community; only the Father and his word can sustain them. Paul's commendation is the apostolic echo of Jesus's intercession.
33: 'I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing.' The apostle's financial integrity in ministry mirrors Jesus's own non-acquisitive mission: 'Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head' (Matthew 8:20). The servant is not above his master in economics: the one sent by the Lord who owned nothing accumulates nothing, trusting the Father who 'feeds the birds of the air' (Matthew 6:26). The servant's integrity vindicates the Lord he represents.
34: 'These hands ministered to my necessities and to those who were with me.' Paul's manual labor — tent-making with Aquila and Priscilla — is the incarnational model of the servant who works within the human condition rather than above it. Jesus, the carpenter's son (Mark 6:3), worked with his hands before his public ministry; Paul continues to work with his hands throughout his ministry. The risen Lord's servant does not stand above the labor of human life.
35: Paul cites a dominical saying — the word of the Lord Jesus that it is more blessed to give than to receive. This agraphon (unwritten saying, not in the canonical Gospels) grounds Paul's financial ethics in Jesus's own teaching. Jesus's earthly practice had modeled this: the giving of the kingdom (Luke 12:32), the giving of self (Mark 10:45), the commendation of the generous widow (Luke 21:1–4). The risen Lord's words — even those not recorded in the canonical Gospels — continue to govern his community's practice.
36: Paul kneels and prays with the Ephesian elders — the posture of deepest supplication before departure. Jesus had knelt in Gethsemane: 'he withdrew from them about a stone's throw, and knelt down and prayed' (Luke 22:41). The departing servant kneels as his Lord knelt — surrendering the community he cannot keep to the Father who can. The Gethsemane posture in the Miletus farewell is Luke's deliberate echo: this too is a garden prayer before a passion journey.
37: The communal weeping and embracing — 'they wept aloud and embraced Paul and kissed him' — echoes the farewell meals of the Last Supper context. The community's grief at the departing leader's loss mirrors the disciples' grief at Jesus's prediction of departure (John 16:6 — 'because I have said these things to you, sorrow has filled your hearts'). The pastoral love that has been formed over three years finds its expression in tears at the farewell — the same love Jesus had demonstrated and predicted would be the disciples' mark.
38: 'Being sorrowful especially because of the word he had spoken, that they would not see his face again.' The grief over finality mirrors the disciples' grief at Jesus's departure: 'You will weep and lament, but the world will rejoice' (John 16:20). The community that has experienced the risen Lord's presence through his servant grieves the servant's departure — not ultimate loss (they have the Spirit and the word) but the particular pastoral relationship that is irreplaceable. The escort to the ship is the farewell of those who know they will not meet again in this world.
